items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.correct: The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries, bounded by a watershed (high ground that separates one basin from another).
items.misconception: A drainage basin is the bowl-shaped area at the mouth of the river.
items.random_wrong: A drainage basin is the underground source of a river.
items.why: Students take 'basin' too literally as a bowl. It is the entire catchment area — often a huge region, not a depression near the river itself.

items.q: How do scientists know current climate change is anthropogenic?
items.correct: Isotopic signatures of CO2 match fossil fuel sources, warming patterns (cooler stratosphere, warmer troposphere) match greenhouse forcing, and natural forcings cannot explain the rate.
items.misconception: Climate change is mainly caused by natural Milankovitch cycles.
items.random_wrong: Climate change is caused by the sun getting hotter.
items.why: Solar output has been flat or slightly declining since 1980 while temperatures rose sharply. Natural cycles operate on tens of thousands of years; the current change is too fast.
It's A slow-moving river of ice. “A type of cloud” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: Glacier ripping rocks from base. “Rocks falling from cliffs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many years ago did the last ice age end (thousands)?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
